result:
I keep thinking about drizzle—how it exists somewhere between rain and fog, too gentle to complain about, too steady to ignore. Yesterday I watched a solitary bicycle courier thread his way through downtown at dusk, wheels whispering over wet asphalt while tinny music leaked from his earbuds. He was in no hurry. Perhaps he sensed the same loose atmosphere that makes cities feel temporary: the streetlights blooming early, the neon bouncing off puddles like secondhand fireworks.  

Inside the café across the road, a woman sketched strangers without lifting her pen, translating curved shoulders and hunched phones into single, unbroken lines. She ruined every face, but the bodies kept their loneliness intact. I liked that. I liked remembering that bodies speak first, faces lie later.  

My own coffee cooled untouched beside a stack of postcards I keep meaning to send but never address. They’re outdated tourist shots of the same streets I walk daily; the irony feels cozy. Occasionally I consider mailing one to myself five years ago: “Stop rehearsing grief, the rain will teach you softness better than any breakup.” But the mailbox intimidates me with its metal certainty, so the cards stay in my bag like wadded possibilities.  

Eventually the courier disappeared down a lane that smelled of boiled cabbage and trumpet valves. The artist closed her notebook. I remained, licking steam off the window until the whole interior blurred. When I left, the drizzle hadn’t stopped; it had merely grown more honest, wetting everything so thoroughly that only the essential remained visible.

Perhaps he sensed the same loose atmosphere that makes cities feel temporary: the streetlights blooming early, the neon bouncing off puddles like secondhand fireworks.  

Inside the café across the road, a woman sketched strangers without lifting her pen, translating curved shoulders and hunched phones into single, unbroken lines. She ruined every face, but the bodies kept their loneliness intact. I liked that. I liked remembering that bodies speak first, faces lie later.  

My own coffee cooled untouched beside a stack of postcards I keep meaning to send but never address. They’re outdated tourist shots of the same streets I walk daily; the irony feels cozy. Occasionally I consider mailing one to myself five years ago: “Stop rehearsing grief, the rain will teach you softness better than any breakup.” But the mailbox intimidates me with its metal certainty, so the cards stay in my bag like wadded possibilities.  

Eventually the courier disappeared down a lane that smelled of boiled cabbage and trumpet valves. The artist closed her notebook. When I left, the drizzle hadn’t stopped; it had merely grown more honest, wetting everything so thoroughly that only the essential remained visible.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
